section.title: The LORD Directs the Steps
section.sectionRationale: Chapter 16 is bracketed by two of the collection's clearest statements of divine sovereignty over human planning, verse 1 and verse 9, and closes on a third, verse 33, forming a chapter wide meditation on how human effort and God's sovereign hand relate. Between these bookends come sayings on the king's justice, verses 10 to 15, wisdom's worth and humility, verses 16 to 22, and speech and patience, verses 23 to 32.
section.thesis: People plan, speak, and act, but the LORD weighs every motive, determines every step, and governs even the fall of the lot, so that true wisdom commits its works to Him rather than trusting purely in its own planning.
section.frameNote: Four movements: human plans and the LORD's sovereign hand, verses 1 to 9; the king's justice and favor, verses 10 to 15; wisdom's worth, humility, and pleasant words, verses 16 to 22; and speech, patience, and the LORD's sovereignty over the lot, verses 23 to 33.

units.label: Human plans and the LORD's sovereign hand

verses.text: The plans of the {{lev|heart}} belong to man, but the reply of the tongue is from the LORD.
All a man's ways are pure in his own eyes, but his motives are {{takan|weighed}} out by the LORD.
Commit your works to the LORD and your plans will be achieved.
The LORD has made everything for His purpose, even the wicked for the day of disaster.
Everyone who is proud in heart is detestable to the LORD; be assured that he will not go unpunished.
By loving devotion and faithfulness iniquity is atoned for, and by the fear of the LORD one turns aside from evil.
When a man's ways please the LORD, He makes even the man's enemies live at peace with him.
Better a little with righteousness than great gain with injustice.
A man's heart plans his course, but the LORD determines his steps.
A divine verdict is on the lips of a king; his mouth must not betray justice.
Honest scales and balances are from the LORD; all the weights in the bag are His concern.
Wicked behavior is detestable to kings, for a throne is established through righteousness.
Righteous lips are a king's delight, and he who speaks honestly is beloved.
The wrath of a king is a messenger of death, but a wise man will pacify it.
When a king's face brightens, there is life; his favor is like a rain cloud in spring.

units.label: Wisdom's worth, humility, and pleasant words

verses.text: How much better to acquire wisdom than gold! To gain understanding is more desirable than silver.
The highway of the upright leads away from evil; he who guards his way protects his life.
{{gaon|Pride}} goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall.
It is better to be lowly in spirit among the humble than to divide the spoil with the proud.
Whoever heeds instruction will find success, and blessed is he who trusts in the LORD.
The wise in heart are called discerning, and pleasant speech promotes instruction.
Understanding is a fountain of life to its possessor, but the discipline of fools is folly.

units.label: Speech, patience, and the LORD over the lot

verses.text: The heart of the wise man instructs his mouth and adds persuasiveness to his lips.
Pleasant words are a honeycomb, sweet to the soul and healing to the bones.
There is a way that seems right to a man, but its end is the way of death.
A worker's appetite works for him because his hunger drives him onward.
A worthless man digs up evil, and his speech is like a scorching fire.
A perverse man spreads dissension, and a gossip divides close friends.
A violent man entices his neighbor and leads him down a path that is not good.
He who winks his eye devises perversity; he who purses his lips is bent on evil.
Gray hair is a crown of glory; it is attained along the path of righteousness.
He who is slow to anger is better than a warrior, and he who controls his temper is greater than one who captures a city.
The {{goral|lot}} is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD.

lev.gloss: The heart, the seat of planning and intention, verse 1. The chapter's opening line concedes that plans belong to the human heart, while insisting the actual outcome of speech and steps ultimately belongs to the LORD, a tension held together rather than resolved into either bare human autonomy or fatalism.

takan.gloss: To weigh, measure, or examine, used in verse 2 of the LORD weighing human motives. The image assumes that self assessment is unreliable, all a man's ways are pure in his own eyes, while God's evaluation, precise as a scale, sees what self examination alone cannot.

gaon.gloss: Pride or haughtiness, named in verse 18 as what goes before destruction, one of the most quoted lines in the book. Its counterpart, lowliness of spirit, verse 19, is preferred even over sharing plunder with the proud, showing humility valued above gain.

goral.gloss: The lot, a method of decision making by chance used throughout the Old Testament, verse 33. Even here, in what looks like pure randomness, the chapter insists every decision is from the LORD, extending His sovereignty to the smallest, most seemingly arbitrary outcomes.

basic.context: This chapter is framed by two nearly matching statements, people plan their course, but the LORD determines the outcome, verses 1 and 9, and closes with a third variation, even the fall of a lot is decided by Him, verse 33. Between these bookends come sayings on kings, wisdom's worth, humility, and self controlled speech, all resting on the same foundation, human effort matters, but it operates under God's sovereign hand.
basic.hermeneutics: The repeated pairing of human planning and divine determination, verses 1, 9, and 33, is the interpretive key to the whole chapter. It teaches neither passive fatalism, since the chapter still commends diligent planning, verse 3, wise speech, and self control, nor human autonomy, since every outcome ultimately belongs to the LORD. Wisdom here means planning and working faithfully while holding the results with open hands.
basic.originalLanguages: Lev, the heart, verse 1, is where plans originate, though their outcome belongs to the LORD. Takan, to weigh, verse 2, pictures God assessing motives with a precision human self judgment cannot match. Gaon, pride, verse 18, gives one of the book's most famous warnings, and goral, the lot, verse 33, shows God's sovereignty reaching even into what looks like pure chance.
basic.typology: The chapter's confidence that the LORD determines every step, verse 9, and every fall of the lot, verse 33, anticipates the New Testament's fuller revelation of God's sovereign plan worked out even through human wickedness, most climactically in the crucifixion, where the very ones who acted freely and wickedly did 'whatever Your hand and Your plan had predestined to take place,' Acts 4:27 to 28.
basic.christ: This chapter's repeated insistence that human plans finally rest in the LORD's hands reaches its deepest and most redemptive expression in the cross. There, wicked men freely planned and carried out Christ's crucifixion, yet the apostles could say they did only 'whatever Your hand and Your plan had predestined to take place,' Acts 4:27 to 28, the same pattern of human intention and divine determination this chapter describes, but now put to the salvation of the world. Committing one's works to the LORD, verse 3, finds its fullest ground in Christ, who committed Himself perfectly into His Father's hands, even in death, Luke 23:46.
basic.god: God weighs every human motive, verse 2, determines every human step, verse 9, and governs even the fall of a lot, verse 33, showing a sovereignty that reaches into the smallest corners of ordinary life. He is also the standard behind just kingship, verses 10 to 15, and the giver of wisdom more valuable than gold, verse 16.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 4:27-28 describes wicked men doing whatever God's hand and plan had predestined, echoing this chapter's pattern of human intention within divine determination. Ephesians 1:11 speaks of God working all things according to the counsel of His will. James 4:13-15 warns against presuming on one's own plans without regard for the Lord's will. Luke 23:46 has Christ commit His spirit into His Father's hands, embodying verse 3's call to commit one's works to the LORD.
basic.oneStory: This chapter's steady insistence that human planning operates entirely within God's sovereign determination belongs to the Bible's larger story of a God who works all things according to His purposes, even human sin and seeming chance. That story reaches its climax at the cross, where human plans of the worst kind became, in God's hand, the very means of salvation, the pattern of this chapter fulfilled at the deepest possible level.

context.literary: A chapter unusually structured around a repeated theological refrain, verses 1, 9, and 33 all restate the same basic truth, human planning under divine determination, giving the chapter more thematic coherence than most in this collection, even while remaining built from independent proverbs.
context.historical: Casting lots, verse 33, was a recognized and legitimate method of decision making in ancient Israel, used for matters ranging from dividing land, Joshua 18:10, to selecting Saul as king, 1 Samuel 10:20-21, reflecting a culture that saw even chance as under God's active governance rather than outside it.
context.canonical: The chapter's pattern of human intention operating within divine sovereignty is echoed throughout Scripture, from Joseph's words to his brothers, you intended evil, but God intended it for good, Genesis 50:20, to the apostles' own account of the crucifixion in Acts 4:27-28, showing this to be a stable, recurring biblical conviction rather than an isolated proverb.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To teach, through a chapter framed by repeated statements of the same truth, that diligent human planning and wise action are genuinely commended even as their final outcome always rests in the LORD's sovereign hand, so that wisdom means working faithfully while trusting God with results.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The command to commit your works to the LORD, verse 3, is directly prescriptive. The theological statements of divine sovereignty, verses 1, 9, 33, are foundational claims about how reality actually works, not merely general life observations, though they function within this book to shape practical trust rather than to settle every philosophical question about the relationship between divine sovereignty and human responsibility.

debates.question: How do verses 1, 9, and 33 relate human freedom and divine sovereignty?

views.label: Compatibilist, both are fully real and coexist without contradiction
views.case: The chapter never denies that people genuinely plan, work, and even cast lots by their own choice, verse 1's 'plans of the heart belong to man,' while affirming God determines the outcome, holding both truths together exactly as Genesis 50:20 and Acts 4:27-28 do elsewhere in Scripture.

views.label: A a call to humble trust rather than a systematic philosophical claim
views.case: As proverbial wisdom rather than doctrinal treatise, the emphasis of these verses is pastoral and practical, teaching humility and trust in planning, rather than resolving the philosophical mechanics of how divine determination and genuine human choice relate.

debates.bearing: Scripture consistently holds genuine human planning and God's sovereign determination together without treating them as contradictory, and this chapter's repeated refrain functions primarily to humble human self confidence and encourage committing plans to the LORD, rather than to settle every question a philosophical theology might ask of it.

study.originalLanguages: Lev, the heart, verse 1, is the true origin of human plans, even while their outcome rests with the LORD. Takan, to weigh, verse 2, pictures God's precise assessment of motives that self judgment alone cannot achieve. Gaon, pride, verse 18, gives the book's most famous warning about the sequence from pride to fall. And goral, the lot, verse 33, shows divine sovereignty reaching into what appears to be pure chance, a conviction reflected in the ancient Israelite practice of casting lots for major decisions.

typology.type: The LORD determines every step and every fall of the lot, Proverbs 16:9, 33
typology.fulfillment: God's sovereign plan worked out even through human wickedness at the cross, Acts 4:27-28.

typology.type: Commit your works to the LORD, Proverbs 16:3
typology.fulfillment: Christ's perfect commitment of Himself into His Father's hands, even in death, Luke 23:46.

study.christ: This chapter's repeated conviction, that human plans and steps ultimately rest in the LORD's hand, verses 1, 9, and 33, reaches its climactic, redemptive expression at the cross. There, as the apostles later prayed, wicked men gathered together to do only 'whatever Your hand and Your plan had predestined to take place,' Acts 4:27 to 28, the very pattern of human intention operating within divine determination this chapter describes, but turned to the salvation of the world. And Christ Himself modeled the chapter's call to commit one's works to the LORD, verse 3, perfectly, entrusting even His own death into His Father's hands, Luke 23:46.
study.god: God weighs every human motive with a precision no self examination can match, verse 2, determines every human step even as people genuinely plan their own course, verse 9, and extends His governance even to the fall of a lot, verse 33. He is the standard beneath just human authority, verses 10 to 15, and the giver of a wisdom more valuable than gold, verse 16.

crossReferences.note: Wicked men did whatever God's hand and plan had predestined, echoing this chapter's pattern of human intention within divine sovereignty, now applied to the cross.
You intended evil against me, but God intended it for good, an earlier instance of the same pattern verses 1, 9, and 33 describe.
God works all things according to the counsel of His will, a New Testament statement of the same sovereignty this chapter affirms.
Father, into Your hands I commit My spirit, embodying the call of verse 3 to commit one's works to the LORD.

study.oneStory: This chapter's steady refrain, that human plans and even the fall of a lot rest finally in God's hands, belongs to Scripture's larger story of a sovereign God who works all things, even human wickedness, toward His good purposes. That story reaches its highest point at the cross, where humanity's worst plan became, in God's hand, the very means of the world's salvation, the pattern this whole chapter had already been describing.

quiz.q: What is the relationship between human planning and divine sovereignty in verses 1 and 9?

options.text: People genuinely plan their course, but the LORD ultimately determines the outcome and the steps.
options.note: Both verses state exactly this, affirming real human planning within God's sovereign determination.

options.text: Human planning is entirely meaningless and has no real role.
options.note: The verses affirm genuine human planning, not its meaninglessness.

options.text: God has no involvement in the outcome of human plans.
options.note: This directly contradicts the verses, which explicitly credit the LORD with determining the outcome.

options.text: Only kings' plans are subject to God's determination.
options.note: The verses speak generally of 'a man,' not a category restricted to kings.

quiz.q: According to verse 2, why does a person need God's evaluation of their motives?

options.text: Because all a man's ways seem pure in his own eyes, but the LORD weighs motives with true accuracy.
options.note: This is the exact reasoning of the verse, exposing the unreliability of self assessment.

options.text: Because human motives are always obviously wicked to everyone, including oneself.
options.note: The verse's point is nearly the opposite, self perception is deceptively favorable, not obviously wicked.

options.text: Because God is uninterested in motives, only actions.
options.note: The verse specifically names motives, not merely outward actions, as what God weighs.

options.text: Because only religious leaders need this kind of evaluation.
options.note: The verse speaks generally of any person's ways and motives, not a specialized class of leaders.

quiz.q: What does verse 18 warn, and how is it usually summarized?

options.text: Pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall.
options.note: This is the exact and famous wording of the verse.

options.text: Pride always leads directly to great success.
options.note: This is the opposite of the verse's actual warning.

options.text: Only kings need to worry about pride.
options.note: The warning is general, applicable to anyone, not restricted to kings.

options.text: Humility is a sign of weakness to be avoided.
options.note: The surrounding verse, 19, actually commends lowliness of spirit as preferable to pride, the opposite of this option.

quiz.q: What does verse 33 say about the casting of lots?

options.text: Even though the lot is cast into the lap, its every decision comes from the LORD.
options.note: This extends divine sovereignty even to what looks like pure chance.

options.text: The casting of lots operates entirely outside of God's control.
options.note: The verse claims the opposite, every decision from the lot is from the LORD.

options.text: Casting lots was forbidden in ancient Israel.
options.note: Casting lots was a recognized and legitimate practice in the Old Testament, not forbidden.

options.text: It applies only to legal disputes in a courtroom.
options.note: The proverb's principle is stated generally about the lot, not restricted to legal contexts alone.

quiz.q: How does this chapter's teaching on divine sovereignty over human plans connect to Christ?

options.text: At the cross, wicked human plans became, under God's sovereign hand, the very means of salvation, as Acts 4:27-28 describes.
options.note: This directly applies the chapter's pattern of human intention within divine determination to the crucifixion.

options.text: It has no connection to the New Testament's account of the cross.
options.note: Acts 4:27-28 explicitly uses this same pattern to describe the crucifixion.

options.text: It teaches that Christ's death was purely accidental and unplanned by God.
options.note: Acts 4:27-28 explicitly says the crucifixion happened according to God's plan, not by accident.

options.text: It shows that human choices played no real role in the crucifixion.
options.note: Acts 4:27-28 affirms both real human intention and divine predetermination together, not the elimination of human choice.

questions: Verse 3 says to commit your works to the LORD. What plan or project are you currently holding so tightly that you have not truly entrusted its outcome to God?
Verse 2 warns that our own ways always seem pure to us. Where might you need someone else's honest perspective because your own self assessment could be off?
Verse 18 warns that pride goes before a fall. Is there an area of confidence in your life that has quietly slipped into pride rather than humble trust?
Verse 24 says pleasant words are like a honeycomb, sweet and healing. Who could use a genuinely kind and encouraging word from you today?
Verse 32 says one who controls his temper is greater than one who captures a city. Where do you most need self control this week, and how might you invite God's help there specifically?

translationNotes.note: This verse can also be rendered 'whoever speaks prudently will find what is good,' a variant reading that shifts the emphasis slightly from heeding instruction to prudent speech.
The phrase describing the worthless man's speech can also be rendered 'and what is on his lips,' a minor variation describing the same destructive, fire like speech.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Proverbs 16:1-33. One em dash present in the source at verse 4 was rewritten using a comma to comply with the no dash rule; the wording is otherwise unchanged and complete.
